messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Help understanding R pkg development policy on patch level
messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel] Help understanding R pkg development policy on patch level
messages.body_plain:
Dear all,

I'm writing you this message since, if possible, I would like to better 
understand a policy behind the development of an R package. For 
simplicity, assume that I'm developing a package that, for some reason, 
requires parsing objects' names in a function call via ...names(). As 
far as I know, the ...names() syntax was introduced in R 4.1.0 and 
received a bug fix in R 4.1.3 
(https://bugs.r-project.org/show_bug.cgi?id=18247). Hence, I believe 
that the minimum R version I should set should be something like 
"Depends: R (>= 4.1.3)". However, the "Writing R Extensions" manual 
(https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html#Package-namespaces) 
says:

 > It is inadvisable to use a dependence on R with patch level (the 
third digit) other than zero. Doing so with packages which others depend 
on will cause the other packages to become unusable under earlier 
versions in the series, and e.g. versions 4.x.1 are widely used 
throughout the Northern Hemisphere academic year.

I don't understand this explanation and I also don't understand why 
specifying R >=? 4.2.0 would be preferable to R >= 4.1.3 when all the 
features I need are already satisfied by R 4.1.3. Can you help? I guess 
that the issue is that, in this case, R 4.1.1 users wouldn't be able to 
install my package, but the same issue persists even if I set R >= 4.2.0 
as the minimum R version. Is that right?

Finally, I also noticed that R CMD check raises a warning in such 
cases?until R 4.3.3.

Just to be clear: this is a very minor issue and, considering how much 
time has passed since the release of those two R versions, I don't see 
any problem with both specifications.

Thanks for your assistance

Andrea

messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel]  Help understanding R pkg development policy on patch level
messages.body_plain:
Andrea,

if the bug affects your use case then you should use 4.1.3 otherwise 4.1.0. Normally, ABI compatibility is guaranteed across all patch versions - hence the comment to make sure the requirement is not unnecessary, but if your package does not work with 4.1.0 but does with 4.1.3 then it's a perfectly justified special case.

Cheers,
Simon

messages.subject_clean: [R-pkg-devel]  Help understanding R pkg development policy on patch level
messages.body_plain:
Andrea,

At this distance in time it is mostly immaterial whether you depend on a patch release. On the other hand, the gain from not just depending on 4.2.0+ must also be negligible by now. 

The reason for the policy was an incident somewhere around R 3.3-3.4, when a relatively minor package (pbkrtest) decided to depend on a patch release (possibly due to an updated sigma() function in 3.4.2, but I may misremember). However, pbkrtest had quite a lot of reverse dependencies, including Rcmdr, which several sites were using for teaching. After that point sites that updated their installation yearly suddenly could not install Rcmdr on their auditorium/computer lab setups, whether or not they actually intenden to use pbkrtest for anything. For the same reason, we have become much more careful not to introduce new functionality in base R between major releases.

-pd

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] CRAN Debian test machine
messages.body_plain:
Hi All:

I have run into this before,  and I have seen others with the same problem.  The machine that CRAN uses for its automatic testing of package submissions appears to be slower for the Debian server than some of the other serves, particularly when web access is involved.  On submission,  the Debian machine shows times for examples that I can? t reproduce on any of the other test machines I use,  and this includes Macbuilder,  WInbuilder,  r-universe and r-hub.  Is there a test machine that is known to have about the same speed and bandwidth as the Debian test machine.  More than happy to test against it,  just need to know where I can point to that will produce about the same results.  Again,  it is not an issue of code failure but rather run time of examples.

Thanks for any help.

-Roy

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] CRAN Debian test machine
messages.body_plain:
Roy,

On 15 May 2026 at 13:04, Roy Mendelssohn - NOAA Federal via R-package-devel wrote:
| I have run into this before,  and I have seen others with the same problem.  The machine that CRAN uses for its automatic testing of package submissions appears to be slower for the Debian server than some of the other serves, particularly when web access is involved.  On submission,  the Debian machine shows times for examples that I can? t reproduce on any of the other test machines I use,  and this includes Macbuilder,  WInbuilder,  r-universe and r-hub.  Is there a test machine that is known to have about the same speed and bandwidth as the Debian test machine.  More than happy to test against it,  just need to know where I can point to that will produce about the same results.  Again,  it is not an issue of code failure but rather run time of examples.

What is your actual question? Comparitive run-time?

Note that there are (per [1] and [2]) about four Debian machines among the
total of current 13 (?) reporting machines (and as with the titanic and
icebergs, there is an unknown (?) and invisible number of extra machines
running the various extra checks):
 
  r-devel-linux-x86_64-debian-clang
  r-devel-linux-x86_64-debian-gcc
  r-patched-linux-x86_64
  r-release-linux-x86_64

One of these one serves as 'gatekeeper' for new uploads. I would assume the
machine suffers under parallel load so questions about run-time may be
difficult to answer.

One of the charms of CRAN is that make their work 'irreproducible' in the
sense that they could, but don't, share their setups in, say, reproducible
Docker setups. But such is life: everybody here is a volunteer, working on
donated free time, and there is always more to do. It is hard to change that,
given mindsets and resources.

Cheers, Dirk  (obviously not speaking for CRAN, or blaming anyone)
 
[1] https://cran.r-project.org/web/checks/check_summary.html
[2] https://cran.r-project.org/web/checks/check_flavors.html

--

On 16 May 2026 at 12:12, Roy Mendelssohn - NOAA Federal wrote:
| yes exactly - whatever is used by CRAN for the automated pre-test on submission.  Not a complaint.  Just would like not to have to keep resubmitting to CRAN because the Debian pre-tests fail.  Would prefer to catch that before submission.  If not, that is fine,  I will just do resubmissions

I mentioned that CRAN does not provide, say, Docker containers.  That said,
rocker/r-base (Debian) and rocker/r2u (Ubuntu) are close. You could use them.

>From an "ideally someone else should take care of it" angle, your best bet is
probably the Linux builds at r-universe which happen to be on Debian and use
similar sets of packages. But again, the exact parameterisation is not the
one CRAN uses for reasons already mentioned. There are a number of tutorials
about how to get going with r-universe, it is very much worth it.

Completely avoiding rejection is hard. I happened to have had issues for the
last few months where the Vienna (Debian) machine was more stringent on URL
checking in pdfs too, and it took some effort to discover that ... the very
variant of qpdf I am using on Ubuntu 25.10 had a change in its command-line
arguments (since reversed, it seems) that influenced the URL extraction.
Sadly even when we try our hardest given the current setup we still do not
come perfectly close. C'est la vie.

| > On May 16, 2026, at 12:06?PM, John Harrold <john.m.harrold at gmail.com> wrote:
| > I think this is the question:
| > 
| > Is there a test machine that is known to have about the same speed and bandwidth as the Debian test machine.

Even if it existed, how would you account for variable, unobservable, and
presumably significant, load on the CRAN machine?  

Dirk

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] CRAN Debian test machine
messages.body_plain:
This may be a dumb question but are the tests meant to test actual resource usage beyond some levels OR meant to allow the existing machines to handle the load?

The former case might measure not elapsed time on a busy machine, but the time-shared time that remains about the same even on a busy machine or the number of bytes transferred over a network rather than how long it took on the clock.

Of course, measuring such things can add an extra layer of overhead so we may be stuck with what is being used, and it can be random whether a tests fails if done during some rush hour versus at 3 AM.

Is this about rerddapUtils [1]? I think that the example timings can be
reproduced on the Mac Builder, it's just that R CMD check runs without
--as-cran, so the NOTE doesn't happen.

On CRAN submission, the package had

>> * checking examples ... [4s/17s] NOTE

On the Mac Builder [2], I see

>> * checking examples ... [0s/40s] OK

Through a cheap proxy in the Netherlands, I can observe

>> * checking examples ... [3s/47s] NOTE
>> Examples with CPU (user + system) or elapsed time > 5s
>>                 user system elapsed
>> griddap_season 1.471  0.099  14.665
>> griddap_split  0.727  0.062  12.058
>> latlon_to_xy   0.053  0.000  10.011
>> xy_to_latlon   0.049  0.000  10.010

Interestingly, latlon_to_xy & xy_to_latlon do really time out for me,
while griddap_season & griddap_split succeed, just take a while.

It's not obvious how to reproduce the NOTE without paying for a proxy
in a different country.

-- 
Best regards,
Ivan

      From: README.md
      Message: Empty URL

Flavor: r-devel-linux-x86_64-debian-gcc, r-devel-windows-x86_64
Check: dependencies in R code, Result: NOTE
  There are ::: calls to the package's namespace in its code. A package
    almost never needs to use ::: for its own objects:
    '.all_args_here_fill' '.base_op_check' '.colons_check_message'
    '.extract_all_fun_names' '.fun_args_pos' '.functions_detect'
    '.has_odd_number_of_quotes' '.in_parenthesis_replacement'
    '.in_quotes_replacement' '.noclean_functions'
    '.pack_and_function_check'


1) I cannot find the empty url in the README document.
2) They are indeed ::: calls to the package's namespace in the code. It is on purpose. It is related to the safer-r project requirements:
https://github.com/safer-r#features-of-the-safer-r-functions
Since this is only NOTES and not ERROR or WARNINGS, I would like to know if the saferDev package can be accepted as it is.

Many thanks for your understanding.

Best,

Gael Millot.

url = "
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/safer-r/saferDev/refs/heads/main/README.md
"
md = tempfile(fileext = ".md")
html = tempfile(fileext = ".html")

download.file(url, md)
tools:::.pandoc_md_for_CRAN(md, html)
# spliced out of tools:::.get_urls_from_HTML_file
html |>
  xml2::read_html() |>
  xml2::xml_find_first("//a") |> # NB: xml_find_all for all URLs
  xml2::xml_parent() # to give some context of where to find it

Mike C

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Two questions about reverse dependencies
messages.body_plain:
Dear R-Dev Community,


I have a social etiquette question and a technical question about reverse
dependency conflicts.

Context note 1: I recently submitted an update for one of my packages on
CRAN, and got back a cautioning email about a strong first order reverse
dependency conflict. I try and do my due diligence (i.e. viz R CMD check
--as-cran, and using all three winbuilder checks, as well as by examining
existing notes for my packages).

Context note 2: The recent reverse dependency conflict was a result of my
adding a new option. If I abstract the nature of this conflict (which
matters to me, because I suppose this can happen to multiple packages), the
former version look something like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3), and my
update looked like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3). Because a package
depending on mine uses example code with implicit argument ordering, my
update breaks their dependency (although all the functionality of the
previous version remains available with explicit argument naming, or
adjustment to implicit argument syntax).

Question 1: Is there a recommended way to communicate to maintainers of
dependent packages changes which may break their examples? Should this be
seen as a negotiation or collaboration, or more as a courtesy? It is quite
easy for me to revise my package update so that it does not create a
conflict by revising to my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3, opt2a), although I
feel this will be 'less logical' in terms of documentation and where one
might expect to see the new functionality of opt2a documented and
implemented.

Question 2: Is there a way for me to check for these kinds of reverse
dependencies as part of my due diligence, and without having to rely on the
CRAN mavens' to catch them for me?


Sincerely,
Alexis

	[[alternative HTML version deleted]]

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Two questions about reverse dependencies
messages.body_plain:
On 2026-05-24 1:39 p.m., Alexis Dinno via R-package-devel wrote:
> Dear R-Dev Community,
> 
> 
> I have a social etiquette question and a technical question about reverse
> dependency conflicts.
> 
> Context note 1: I recently submitted an update for one of my packages on
> CRAN, and got back a cautioning email about a strong first order reverse
> dependency conflict. I try and do my due diligence (i.e. viz R CMD check
> --as-cran, and using all three winbuilder checks, as well as by examining
> existing notes for my packages).
> 
> Context note 2: The recent reverse dependency conflict was a result of my
> adding a new option. If I abstract the nature of this conflict (which
> matters to me, because I suppose this can happen to multiple packages), the
> former version look something like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3), and my
> update looked like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3). Because a package
> depending on mine uses example code with implicit argument ordering, my
> update breaks their dependency (although all the functionality of the
> previous version remains available with explicit argument naming, or
> adjustment to implicit argument syntax).
> 
> Question 1: Is there a recommended way to communicate to maintainers of
> dependent packages changes which may break their examples? Should this be
> seen as a negotiation or collaboration, or more as a courtesy? It is quite
> easy for me to revise my package update so that it does not create a
> conflict by revising to my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3, opt2a), although I
> feel this will be 'less logical' in terms of documentation and where one
> might expect to see the new functionality of opt2a documented and
> implemented.

First, I would advise you against the change you made.  If you're adding 
a new parameter to a function, the safest way to do so is to add it 
after all the other parameters, and set a default value so the old usage 
will still produced the same results.

That is, your new function should have a definition like

   my.function <- function(opt1, opt2, opt3, opt2a = "old option value") 
{ ... }

so that any call using only the 3 old parameters will produce the same 
result in the old version and the new one.  Then you don't need to worry 
about communicating with the maintainers of reverse dependencies, 
because you didn't break anything.

More importantly, you also won't break scripts that your users put 
together using the old function definition.  You have no way of knowing 
about those unless those users tell you that you've messed up their 
workflow.  So be nice to your users, and don't cause them trouble.

Sometimes this is impossible, i.e. the new change fixes some issue and 
can't be made automatically compatible with the old one.  Then the best 
solution is to change the name of the function, and redefine 
`my.function` to a wrapper for the new one.

If you don't want to do that, then you need to communicate with the 
package maintainers and with your users about how to fix things.

You can find the list of reverse dependencies from the CRAN page for 
your package, or automatically using the tools::dependsOnPkgs function, 
or various other functions from contributed packages.

You should write to each of the maintainers, telling them about the 
change and what they need to do to remain compatible.  If you want to be 
nice, you can examine each of those packages and put together a patch to 
fix the change, but CRAN doesn't expect that much work.

Communicating with your users is harder.  One way to include code in the 
new function that tries to detect if it is being called using the old 
convention, and if so, immediately fail with an informative message.

> 
> Question 2: Is there a way for me to check for these kinds of reverse
> dependencies as part of my due diligence, and without having to rely on the
> CRAN mavens' to catch them for me?

See above.

Duncan Murdoch

> 
> 
> Sincerely,
> Alexis
>

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Two questions about reverse dependencies
messages.body_plain:
Alexis,

I like your question and it is indeed a barrier to make changes to your
functions if they require others outside your control to adjust.

I suggest you may consider another choice for your function argument you
call opt2a that does not break things.

my.function(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3)

First, ask yourself if opt2a is needed each time or can default to
something. If it can be say NA, then code like this which moves it to a
non-positional argument may work for you:

my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3, opt2a=NA)

The person using the old version of the function can call it with the usual
three arguments. If they want to use the new and enhanced version, they add
a fourth argument.

The other solution at times can be to write a second function  like:

my.function4(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3)

Then simply change your earlier function to look like this:

my.function <- function(opt1, opt2, opt3) { 
opt2a = NA # or whatever your default is such as a copy of op2
my.function4(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3)
}


The above would work for many cases unless you are playing with things like
deferred evaluation. Older users can keep using their code in your new
package, albeit a tad more slowly with the extra function call. New users
can simply start using my.function4() and the old function may eventually be
deprecated.

Your situation, of course, may not allow this if the new argument is not
only required but has no default.


-----Original Message-----
From: R-package-devel <r-package-devel-bounces at r-project.org> On Behalf Of
Alexis Dinno via R-package-devel
Sent: Sunday, May 24, 2026 1:40 PM
To: List r-package-devel <r-package-devel at r-project.org>
Subject: [R-pkg-devel] Two questions about reverse dependencies

Dear R-Dev Community,


I have a social etiquette question and a technical question about reverse
dependency conflicts.

Context note 1: I recently submitted an update for one of my packages on
CRAN, and got back a cautioning email about a strong first order reverse
dependency conflict. I try and do my due diligence (i.e. viz R CMD check
--as-cran, and using all three winbuilder checks, as well as by examining
existing notes for my packages).

Context note 2: The recent reverse dependency conflict was a result of my
adding a new option. If I abstract the nature of this conflict (which
matters to me, because I suppose this can happen to multiple packages), the
former version look something like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3), and my
update looked like my.function(opt1, opt2, opt2a, opt3). Because a package
depending on mine uses example code with implicit argument ordering, my
update breaks their dependency (although all the functionality of the
previous version remains available with explicit argument naming, or
adjustment to implicit argument syntax).

Question 1: Is there a recommended way to communicate to maintainers of
dependent packages changes which may break their examples? Should this be
seen as a negotiation or collaboration, or more as a courtesy? It is quite
easy for me to revise my package update so that it does not create a
conflict by revising to my.function(opt1, opt2, opt3, opt2a), although I
feel this will be 'less logical' in terms of documentation and where one
might expect to see the new functionality of opt2a documented and
implemented.

Question 2: Is there a way for me to check for these kinds of reverse
dependencies as part of my due diligence, and without having to rely on the
CRAN mavens' to catch them for me?


Sincerely,
Alexis

messages.subject: [R-pkg-devel] Two questions about reverse dependencies
messages.body_plain:
? Sun, 24 May 2026 10:39:36 -0700
Alexis Dinno via R-package-devel <r-package-devel at r-project.org> ?????:

> Is there a way for me to check for these kinds of reverse
> dependencies as part of my due diligence, and without having to rely
> on the CRAN mavens' to catch them for me?

The base R tools for this are tools::package_dependencies(reverse=TRUE,
which = "most", recursive = "strong") to get the list of dependencies,
utils::download.packages(...) to obtain their latest tarballs and
tools::check_packages_in_dir() to check them (with the new version of
your package installed).

Once the list of packages to check gets into hundreds, some automation
becomes necessary to schedule the checks to run in parallel, sometimes
for more than a day, and to maintain their dependency trees as well:

https://CRAN.R-project.org/package=prrd
https://github.com/r-lib/revdepcheck
https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/xfun/refman/xfun.html#rev_check

You'll need to install 436 packages, but run only 23 checks.

-- 
Best regards,
Ivan
